text.title: ruby-rspec-testing Failure Modes
text.summary: Migrated antipatterns for ruby-rspec-testing as failure modes. Add detector + repair clauses where missing.

failure-mode.description: Emitting deprecated RSpec idioms: `it { should validate_presence_of(:email) }`, controller specs with `assigns(:user)`, `RSpec.configure` with deprecated config keys, `assert_template`.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: RSpec 3.0+ deprecates the `should` syntax (pre-RSpec-3 idiom) and controller specs in favor of request specs. Agents trained on older tutorials or Rails 4-era documentation emit these patterns. Always enforce the modern form: `is_expected.to`, request specs, `have_http_status` matchers.

failure-mode.description: Using `create(:model)` everywhere instead of `build_stubbed` for specs that don't query the database. Creates 1000s of database rows in test runs.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Multiplies test duration; CI becomes slow and CI quota is exceeded. Force the rule in the prompt: "if the test doesn't query the DB, use build_stubbed". For model specs testing validations, use `is_expected.to` matchers instead of building invalid records.

failure-mode.description: Hidden test-order coupling via shared state: `@user = create(:user)` in `before(:all)`, or lazy `let` accessing instance variables set in another context. Tests pass in sequence but fail on `--order random`.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: RSpec random order (enable in `.rspec` with `--order random`) surfaces coupling early. Lazy `let` avoids this; use eager `let!` only when side effects are needed. Human-in-loop: run tests with `--order random --seed 1234` before merge; if order-dependent, force fix in the agent loop.

failure-mode.description: Hard-coding timestamps: `Time.current`, `Date.today` in spec body. Tests fail on Feb 29, DST transitions, or month boundaries without human notice.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Use `travel_to(Time.zone.local(2026, 5, 1, 12, 0, 0))` to freeze time explicitly. Reset with `travel_back` in an `after` block. Never rely on the current date/time in a test.

failure-mode.description: Adding `sleep` or `wait_for_ajax` loops in system specs instead of using Capybara matchers (`have_content`, `have_selector`).
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Capybara's matchers auto-wait with sensible defaults (2-30s configurable); `sleep` is unreliable on CI with variable load and adds unnecessary latency. Use Capybara's built-in waits; only add custom `wait_for_ajax` if the app is known to use AJAX and Capybara can't detect it.

failure-mode.description: Defining factories inside spec files (`FactoryBot.define { factory :user do ... end }` in `_spec.rb`) instead of under `spec/factories/`.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Duplicate factories across files, harder to reuse and maintain. Move all factories to `spec/factories/`, one file per model. Agents often flatten factory hierarchies; enforce inheritance and traits in review.

failure-mode.description: VCR cassettes with `record: :all` or `record: :new_episodes` in CI, allowing silent re-record when APIs change. Cassettes encode API behavior at moment-of-record forever.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Accidental re-record in CI masks API changes. Force `record: :none` in CI; local development can use `record: :new_episodes`, and cassettes are committed for review before merge. Human decision gate: "does the cassette change match the API change?".

failure-mode.description: Mocking what you don't own: `allow_any_instance_of(HTTP::Client)` mocks the lib, diverging from reality. When the lib changes, mocks don't.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Force a `RemoteService` wrapper class and stub that boundary. Mocking the third-party lib directly hides integration bugs.

failure-mode.description: System spec flake from Capybara waits tuning. Tests pass locally but flake on CI due to `Capybara.default_max_wait_time` being too short or not set.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Capybara's default is 2 seconds. On slow CI (shared runners), increase to 5-10 seconds. Configure in `spec/support/capybara.rb` or `rails_helper.rb`. Use `Capybara.current_session.config.default_max_wait_time = 10` to set globally.

failure-mode.description: DatabaseCleaner strategy set globally to `:truncation` instead of context-specific `:transaction`. Truncation is slow and unnecessary for non-JS tests.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Configure DatabaseCleaner: use `:transaction` for normal model/request specs (fast, atomic); switch to `:truncation` only for system specs that use Selenium/Capybara (JS-driven, require a separate connection). Global `:truncation` doubles CI time.

failure-mode.description: Skipping flaky specs to make CI green without investigation or follow-up. Disabled tests become stale and are never re-enabled.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Flaky specs signal a real bug: ordering dependency, timing issue, or test environment problem. Disable with a human TODO: "fix by X date". Set a calendar reminder to re-enable and investigate.
